manifest.name: agile-testing
manifest.title: Agile Testing
manifest.subtitle: A Practical Guide for Testers and Agile Teams — Gregory & Crispin (2009)
manifest.home-description: A Practical Guide for Testers and Agile Teams — Gregory & Crispin (2009)

section.paragraph: Agile testing is not just testing on an agile project. It encompasses business-facing tests that define features, tests that critique the product, and everything beyond unit/component testing: functional, system, load, performance, security, stress, usability, exploratory, end-to-end, and user acceptance testing.

Core practices include test-driven development (TDD), where the programmer writes a test for a tiny bit of functionality, sees it fail, writes the code that makes it pass, then moves on. Programmers also write integration tests to ensure small units work together.

glossary.definition: Business experts, product owners, domain experts, analysts — everyone on the "business" side. They write stories, provide examples, and collaborate throughout each iteration.
Everyone involved with delivering code: programmers, sysadmins, architects, DBAs, testers. Agile principles encourage team members to take on multiple activities.
Tests that define the business experts' desired features and functionality, expressed in domain language. Synonym: customer-facing tests.
Tests described in programmer terms and jargon, falling into the programmer's domain (coined by Brian Marick).

list.item: Testing happens at the end, right before release
Testing gets "squished" because coding takes longer than expected
Requirements are frozen; changes deferred to next release
Testers study requirements docs, write test plans, then wait for work
Testers act as gatekeepers of quality
Long development cycles (months to a year)
Focused on conformance to requirements
Testing is iterative and incremental — each increment tested as soon as finished
Story is not "done" until it has been tested
Requirements evolve; changes welcomed between iterations
Tests written collaboratively days or hours before coding begins
Whole team responsible for quality
Short iterations (1-4 weeks)
Focused on delivering business value and customer-needed quality

section.paragraph: The biggest difference in agile vs. traditional development. Quality is not owned by testers alone — the entire team feels responsible for delivering high-quality software.

list.item: Everyone on the team gets "test-infected"
Tests from unit level up drive coding, help learn how the app should work, and signal when a task is "done"
Any task can be completed by any team member or pair
The whole team thinks constantly about designing for testability
Constant collaboration: testers with programmers, customers, and specialists
Anyone can ask for and receive help
It's all about quality — not speed

key-box.heading: Critical Insight
key-box.paragraph: Some folks new to agile perceive it as all about speed. The fact is, it's all about quality — and if it's not, question whether it's really an "agile" team.

section.paragraph: An agile tester doesn't see herself as a quality police officer. She's ready to gather and share information, help customers express requirements, and provide feedback on progress.

list.item: Customer-focused — sees the system from the user's viewpoint
Results-oriented — passionate about delivering business value
Craftsman-like — takes pride in quality work
Collaborative — works with everyone on the team
Eager to learn — continually grows professionally
Creative — open to ideas, willing to take on any task

section.paragraph: "Without the attitude, the skill is nothing." — Janet Gregory

key-box.heading: 1. Provide Continuous Feedback
key-box.paragraph: The tester's role as "information provider" is inherently valuable. Help the product owner articulate requirements as examples and tests. Run tests early and often for meaningful feedback. Use big visible charts to display progress.

key-box.heading: 2. Deliver Value to the Customer
key-box.paragraph: Focus on delivering the most critical functionality first. Identify the "thin slice" of necessary functionality, complete those tasks first, then flesh out the rest. Avoid scope creep — an innocent request can add a lot of complexity.

key-box.heading: 3. Enable Face-to-Face Communication
key-box.paragraph: Use the "Power of Three" rule: all discussions about a feature need a programmer, a tester, and the product owner. Testers help customers and developers achieve a common language. Use examples to develop this shared language.

key-box.heading: 4. Have Courage
key-box.paragraph: Courage to fail fast and learn. Courage to let others make mistakes. Courage to ask for help. Courage to climb out of your silo and join team responsibility for success or failure.

key-box.heading: 5. Keep It Simple
key-box.paragraph: Do the simplest thing that could possibly work. Test "just enough" with the lightest-weight tools. Push tests to the lowest level possible for fast feedback. Time-box exploratory testing side trips.

key-box.heading: 6. Practice Continuous Improvement
key-box.paragraph: Participate in retrospectives. Keep an "impediment backlog." Try new tools and practices. Short iterations make it easy to experiment. Learn new skills — attend user groups, read articles, join mailing lists.

key-box.heading: 7. Respond to Change
key-box.paragraph: Welcome change even when it's uncomfortable. Automated testing is the key to handling changing requirements. No agile team will succeed doing only manual testing.

key-box.heading: 8. Self-Organize
key-box.paragraph: The team creates its own approach and commits to it. When the team creates the plan, members adopt a new attitude toward testing. The highest-priority issues are problems for the whole team to solve.

key-box.heading: 9. Focus on People
key-box.paragraph: Projects succeed when good people are allowed to do their best work. Agile teams give all members equal weight. Testing knowledge is one component of the team's ability to deliver value.

key-box.heading: 10. Enjoy
key-box.paragraph: Working on a collaborative team where everyone takes responsibility for quality is a tester's Utopia. Agile development rewards the agile tester's passion for her work.

list.item: Think from both stakeholder and technical perspectives
Help customers articulate requirements as tests
Raise questions that flush out hidden assumptions
Consider non-functional requirements (performance, security, reliability)
Keep the testing approach simple and lightweight
Verify minimum testing was completed by end of iteration

key-box.heading: Peril: Not Being Part of the Team
key-box.paragraph: If you're not invited to planning sessions, stand-ups, or design meetings — or if you attend but don't speak up — your team is at risk. Hidden assumptions go undetected, ripple effects are missed, and communication breaks down. Invite yourself to meetings, sit near developers, pair test, and make yourself indispensable.

section.paragraph: Organizational culture is defined by its values, norms, and assumptions. It governs how people communicate, interrelate, and make decisions. Culture too frequently is not considered when implementing agile, leaving people wondering why it didn't work.

list.item: Does the organization tolerate poor quality or value it?
If any one group has assumed ownership of quality, they must learn to share it
Whole-team ownership of quality requires a mind shift from "owning" to "participating"

section.paragraph: Work at a consistent pace that sustains constant velocity. A 40-hour week is normal for XP teams. If overtime is needed, the whole team should work extra, not just testers.
Move from vendor-supplier to partnership. Agile depends on close customer involvement. Have representative domain experts while keeping all stakeholders informed.

section.h2: 3.2 Barriers to Agile Adoption by Test/QA Teams

row.cell: Fear of losing QA identity, job security, support structure when dispersed into dev teams
Missing specialists or expertise key to success; need time and training to understand new roles
Testers put into dev units without training — many quit within months
"Mini-waterfall" — squeezing traditional SDLC phases into short iterations; same problems, faster
"Methodology du jour" fatigue; reverting to bad habits under stress
Each role transitions from a different perspective; need rules and guidelines to communicate

list.item: Right to bring up testing, quality, and process issues at any time
Right to ask questions and receive timely answers
Right to ask for and receive help from anyone on the project
Right to estimate testing tasks included in story estimates
Right to the tools needed to perform testing tasks
Right to expect the entire team to be responsible for quality
Talk about fears — use retrospectives; teach that it's acceptable to be fearful
Give team ownership — let the team customize its approach; self-organizing teams make better plans
Celebrate success — acknowledge every achievement, however small
Be patient — help the team make baby steps; focus on tiny positive increments
Let them feel pain — when suggestions are rebuffed and the team fails, bring them up again
Build credibility — show value by going to programmers with issues directly, not via bug reports
Professional development — learn new tools, languages, attend conferences
Beware quality police mentality — be a collaborator, not an enforcer
Vote with your feet — if nothing works despite best efforts, find a better team

section.h4: Independent QA Teams vs. Integrated Teams
section.paragraph: Teams often confuse "independent" with "separate." Separate reporting, budgets, and processes create division, friction, and an "us vs. them" attitude. Instead, think of a community of testers — a learning organization for career development and sharing ideas, while testers are integrated into project teams.

list.item: Testers need training on pair testing, working with incomplete requirements, and automation
It can take up to six months for testers to feel confident with the new process
Pairing programmers and testers improves communication about product quality
One budget, one schedule — no "testing time" to cut if functionality isn't finished
Testing tasks must be given the same attention as coding tasks

section.paragraph: There is no "right" ratio. It depends on application complexity, tester skill set, and tools used. Ratios from 1:20 to 1:1 have all worked. Rather than focus on a ratio, evaluate the testing skills needed and find appropriate resources.

list.item: Attitude is more important than specific technical skills
Look for: collaboration, exploratory testing, scripting experience, self-organizing ability
Screen out "quality police" mentality
Passion for learning is paramount
Post in less obvious places (agile user groups, language mailing lists)
Self-organizing teams make the best progress when empowered to solve their own problems
Every member has equal value — testers must be invited to all meetings; if forgotten, invite yourself
Performance and rewards should promote team collaboration, not undermine it
Celebrate every success — gold stars, high-fives, team lunches
Co-locate teams when possible; use candy dishes and creative approaches to bridge physical gaps
Cycle time — time from concept to cash; focus on repeatedly and reliably delivering value
Financial return — measure ROI to ensure best value delivery
Customer delight — simple ways to measure whether customers are delighted
As guideposts — telling the team when it's getting off track or on the right track
To track milestones toward team goals
As a motivating force (e.g., celebrating test count milestones)
Big visible charts are the most effective way to display metrics
Don't use metrics to judge individual or team performance
Don't measure for the sake of producing numbers
Lines of code, defect counts per person — these can be twisted into any interpretation
Figure each metric's ROI: does the effort of collecting justify the value?
Convenience — details, screen prints, supplemental docs in one place
Knowledge base — look up old issues and their resolutions
Intermittent bugs — capture investigation progress over time
Large/distributed teams — shared visibility across teams
Customer support — track what was fixed in each release
Doesn't promote direct communication between programmers and testers
Writing detailed steps is time-consuming waste
Defects become inventory — a "secret backlog"
How much easier to just talk to the programmer and show the bug

key-box.heading: Keep Your Focus
key-box.paragraph: The main target is delivering the best quality product with value to the business. If talking and fixing right away works, do that. If you need a DTS for analysis, use it. Whatever works for your whole team.

section.paragraph: Test Strategy = long-term, static document that seldom changes. Covers overall test approach: practices, automation, environments, tools. Good for compliance and onboarding.
Test Plan = unique to each project. The power of planning is identifying issues, dependencies, and risks — not producing a document. Whether you create a document or not, the planning must be done.

Traceability in agile: build functionality in tiny steps, work closely together, test each story as it's coded. Link source code check-in comments to tests or requirements. Tools like FitNesse integrate tests directly with requirements.

Audits (SAS 70, SOX), CMMI, and ITIL can coexist with agile. Write story cards for audit support activities. Use retrospectives for constant process improvement (maps to CMMI). Separate measurement goals from means to improve those measurements.

chapter.title: The Purpose of Testing — Agile Testing Quadrants

section.paragraph: The quadrant numbering does not imply order of execution. Timing depends on project risks, customer goals, legacy vs. greenfield, and resource availability.

section.h2: 6.2 Tests that Support the Team (Q1 & Q2)

list.item: Unit tests and component tests via TDD
Automated with xUnit family tools
Measure internal quality of code
Run with every code check-in for instant feedback
Not intended for customer use
Define external quality and desired features
Derived from customer examples; written in domain language
Each test verifies a business satisfaction condition
Most should be automated for quick feedback
Includes mock-ups and wireframes for GUI validation

section.h2: 6.3 Tests that Critique the Product (Q3 & Q4)

list.item: Emulate how a real user would work the application
Exploratory testing, usability testing, scenario testing, UAT
Manual testing that only a human can do
Many of the most serious bugs are found here
Performance, load, stress, security, scalability, "ility" testing
May require specialized tools and expertise
Should be considered at every step, not left until the end
Often more important than functionality (e.g., 1-minute response time kills UX)

section.h2: 6.4 Knowing When a Story Is Done
section.paragraph: Use all four quadrants as a checklist. Use "stock" cards on the story board (unit test card, end-to-end card, security card, UI review card) to ensure all types are considered. The combination of tests from all four quadrants lets the team know when each feature has met the customer's criteria.

key-box.heading: Managing Technical Debt
key-box.paragraph: Technical debt builds up when teams take shortcuts, skip tests, or hack quick fixes. Each quadrant plays a role in keeping it manageable. By minimizing debt, a team has time and resources for quality testing.

list.item: The value of any practice depends on its context
Good practices in context, but no best practices
People working together are the most important part of any project
Projects unfold in often unpredictable ways
The product is a solution — if the problem isn't solved, the product doesn't work
Good software testing is a challenging intellectual process

chapter.title: Technology-Facing Tests that Support the Team

section.paragraph: Without TDD, automated unit/component tests, and continuous integration, it's hard to deliver value in a timely manner. All other quadrants can't make up for inadequacies here.

list.item: TDD is more about design than testing — code developed test-first is naturally designed for testability
Minimizes bugs that must be caught later
When post-development testing is occupied with unit-level bugs, there's no time for serious business issues
Go faster and do more — speed is a long-term side effect of high internal quality
Make testers' jobs easier — testers waste little time finding low-level bugs; can focus on exploratory testing and higher-level scenarios
Design for testability — layered architectures, ports and adapters pattern, mock objects
Timely feedback — continuous build should finish within 10 minutes
Use fake/mock objects instead of actual databases at unit level
Move longer-running tests to a secondary build process
Run tests in parallel
Use standard, unchanging test data sets
Distribute tasks across multiple build machines
Upgrade hardware and software
Strangler application — code new stories test-first in new architecture while maintaining old system; gradually convert
Legacy code rescue — Michael Feathers' techniques from Working Effectively with Legacy Code
Test "minutes afterward" when test-first isn't possible
Let agile principles guide your approach to enabling automated testing

section.h2: 7.4 What If the Team Doesn't Do These Tests?

list.item: Testers: use change patterns from Fearless Change — "Ask for Help," "Brown Bag" sessions, "Guru on Your Side"
Managers: make quality the goal; provide expert training; budget time for refactoring and tool upgrades
Team: raise issues at retrospectives; suggest experimenting for a few iterations
Don't have testers write unit tests — TDD is a design activity for the person writing the code

row.cell: Git, SVN, Perforce — essential for any development; include test scripts in version control
IntelliJ, Eclipse, VS Code — support refactoring, TDD, integration with source control
Ant, Maven, Gradle, Nant — create deployable packages
Jenkins, Hudson, Bamboo, TeamCity — run tests on every check-in
JUnit, NUnit, pytest, RSpec — language-specific xUnit family

chapter.title: Business-Facing Tests that Support the Team

section.paragraph: Stories are only a starting point for a conversation. Business-facing tests address requirements, provide the big picture and enough detail to guide coding, and express requirements in a language both customer and dev teams understand.
The formula: Story + Conversation + Examples = Tests that drive coding

list.item: What problem are we trying to solve?
How will the story bring value to the business?
Who are the end users? What value will they get?
What's the worst thing that could happen? (generates risk-based tests)
What's the best thing that could happen? (generates happy path tests)
How do we know we're done?

section.paragraph: Ask the customer to draw pictures, provide concrete data, show how features should look. Examples form the basis for tests. Capture examples in executable format.

list.item: Business satisfaction conditions
Impact on existing functions (website, documents, reports)
Legal considerations
Data migration needs
Internal and external communications

section.paragraph: Start with the "thin slice" (steel thread, tracer bullet) — the most stripped-down happy path from end to end. After it works, add layers of functionality incrementally.
The pattern: write tests → write code → run tests → learn, building on each pass.

key-box.heading: Ripple Effects
key-box.paragraph: Each story drops like a stone into the application. Make a list of all parts of the system that might be affected. Check each "test point" for additional requirements and test cases. A small story might have wide-ranging impact.

list.item: Start with the happy path, then define highest-risk scenarios
Ask programmers about post conditions, fragile code areas, and system interactions
Don't get so involved in detailed test cases that you lose the big picture
Time-box the time spent writing tests before coding starts

row.cell: Product owner story checklists covering satisfaction conditions, impacts, legal, data migration
Brainstorm ideas, explore feature implications on whiteboard or paper
Define examples using tools familiar to business domain (especially financial calculations)
Paper prototypes, whiteboard drawings, modified existing screenshots
Capture workflows and decision trees; tie multiple stories together
Share test cases, decisions, mock-ups; co-locate narrative and executable tests

list.item: Fit / FitNesse — customers specify expectations in tables; programmers write fixtures; color-coded results
BDD tools (easyb, RSpec, JBehave) — use "should" language; executable documentation in domain language
xUnit tools — some teams use JUnit/NUnit for business-facing tests with custom frameworks on top
Selenium — HTML tables or coded in multiple languages; runs in real browsers
Watir — Ruby library for automating browsers; supports DSL creation
Canoo WebTest — XML-specified steps; no logic means you don't have to test the test
Home-brewed frameworks — built on open source tools; tailored to exact project needs
Build incrementally — start with simple happy path, add complexity after it passes
Keep tests passing — once a test passes, it shouldn't fail unless requirements change; fix broken builds immediately
Use design patterns — Build/Operate/Check, time-based, event-based patterns
Keyword and data-driven — pre-defined action words + data tables for maintainable, reusable tests
One business rule per test — confine each test to one condition

key-box.heading: Testability
key-box.paragraph: Code design and test design are complementary and interdependent. You can't write tests without testable code, and you can't write code without well-designed tests. Always consider coding and testing together.

chapter.title: Business-Facing Tests that Critique the Product

section.paragraph: No matter how hard we try to get it right, people make mistakes. Critiquing is constructive — reviewing software to learn how to improve it. This is primarily manual testing that only a human can do.
You won't have time for Q3 tests if you haven't automated Q1 and Q2.

list.item: Show customers what you're developing early and often
End-of-iteration demos let business users revise priorities
Informal demos during development are even more productive
"Soap opera testing" — real-life scenarios, exaggerated and compressed into quick sequences
Use realistic data from customers; trace data flow through the system
Flow diagrams help identify common scenarios and test end-to-end

section.paragraph: Simultaneous test design, execution, and learning. Not ad hoc — it's a sophisticated, thoughtful approach. Combines learning, test design, and test execution into one approach.

list.item: Starts with a charter defining what to explore
Requires critical thinking, interpreting results, following "smells"
Take notes for reproducibility
Emphasizes variation over repetition
Session-based testing provides structure with time-boxed sessions and charters
Reveals areas needing more automated tests and generates new story ideas
Navigation, tabbing order, workflow intuitiveness
Wizard of Oz testing — paper prototypes with a human "playing the computer"
Check out the competition for comparison

chapter.title: Critiquing the Product Using Technology-Facing Tests

row.cell: Test early; threat modeling, penetration testing, authentication, authorization, data protection
Code readability, documentation, modular design, test coverage
Integration with other systems, API compatibility, data exchange formats
Browser versions, OS platforms, device types, backward compatibility
Uptime requirements, failure recovery, concurrent user handling
Installation scripts, upgrades, rollback procedures, configuration

list.item: Scalability — can the architecture handle growth? Test early with production-level loads
Performance and load — establish baselines; automate with tools (JMeter, etc.)
Know nonfunctional requirements before coding — easier to design with them in mind
Don't leave load/usability testing to the end when it might be too late
Use TDD to check nonfunctional requirements (e.g., capacity) for quick feedback

key-box.heading: Test Environments
key-box.paragraph: Performance tests need environments that mirror production as closely as possible. Memory management, database performance, and network conditions all affect results.

section.paragraph: A system test example demonstrates how all quadrants work together in practice:

list.item: Q1: Unit tests (TDD), component tests — automated, fast feedback
Q2: Acceptance tests from customer examples — automated functional tests, API tests, web services tests
Q3: Exploratory testing, scenario testing, data feed testing, end-to-end tests, UAT, documentation review
Q4: Reliability, performance testing — tools and automation
Use as a checklist to ensure all testing bases are covered
Document test code and report results clearly
Examine: Are we using unit tests for design? Do business-facing tests match expectations? Are we budgeting time for exploratory testing? Do we consider performance and security early enough?
Experiment and use retrospectives to keep improving

chapter.title: Why Automate and What Holds Us Back

list.item: Manual testing takes too long — can't keep up with short iterations
Manual processes are error prone — humans make mistakes in repetitive tasks
Frees people for their best work — testers focus on exploratory and high-value testing
Automated regression tests provide a safety net — courage to refactor and change
Early and frequent feedback — know immediately when something breaks
Tests are great documentation — executable, always up-to-date specification
ROI and payback — initial investment pays off over many iterations

row.cell: Show ROI; demonstrate time saved on regression
Invest in training; pair with experienced automators
Start small; build incrementally; demonstrate value early
Push tests to lower levels; use stable APIs
Strangler pattern; start with smoke tests; add coverage gradually
Start with simplest tests; celebrate early wins
Use retrospectives; let team feel the pain of manual testing

section.paragraph: The pyramid guides the proportion of tests at each level:

list.item: Base (largest): Unit and component tests — fast, cheap, numerous
Middle: API/integration tests — test business logic without GUI
Top (smallest): GUI tests — slow, expensive, brittle but necessary

section.paragraph: Deviating from the pyramid increases cost and maintenance burden.

list.item: CI builds and deploys
Unit and component tests
API / web services tests
Tests behind the GUI
GUI smoke tests and critical paths
Load tests
Repetitive tasks
Data creation and setup
Comparisons (expected vs. actual)
Usability testing — requires human judgment
Exploratory testing — requires human intellect
Tests that will never fail — waste of effort
One-off tests — ROI doesn't justify
Where does it hurt most? — automate the biggest pain points first
Multi-layered approach — unit, API, GUI layers with appropriate tools at each
Think about test design and maintenance — design patterns, DSL, modularity
Choose the right tools — based on team skills, technology, budget, not the latest fad
Keep it simple — simplest tests that verify functionality
Whole-team approach — programmers help automate; testers help design
Apply agile coding practices to tests — refactor, TDD for test code, code reviews
Use data generation tools for test data
Avoid database access in unit tests — use mocks/fakes for speed
When database access is unavoidable, use dedicated test schemas with canonical data
Keep test data unchanging and canonical for reliable regression tests
Organize tests — by feature, layer, or test type; include in source code control
Organize results — color-coded pass/fail; email notifications; big visible charts
Tests are code — apply the same standards: refactoring, reviews, version control

chapter.title: Tester Activities in Release or Theme Planning

list.item: Testers contribute to story sizing by identifying testing complexity and effort
Consider testing implications when prioritizing: risk, dependencies, third-party involvement
Focus on value — what delivers the most business value first?
System-wide impact: think about how stories affect the larger application
Types of testing needed (functional, performance, security, etc.)
Infrastructure requirements (test environments, test data)
Test results tracking and communication
Lightweight alternatives: test matrices, spreadsheets, whiteboards, automated test lists

key-box.heading: Visibility and Communication
key-box.paragraph: Track test tasks and status on the story board. Communicate test results with big visible charts. Release metrics (burndown charts, test coverage) help the team and management gauge progress.

list.item: Advance clarity — help customers prepare examples and conditions of satisfaction before planning
Customers speak with one voice — resolve conflicting requirements early
Story size — help break stories into appropriate sizes
Examples — gather examples that will become tests
Consider test strategies, prioritize defects, identify needed resources

key-box.heading: Benefits vs. Risks of Advance Preparation
key-box.paragraph: Benefits: faster iteration start, fewer surprises, better estimates. Risks: wasted time if stories are re-prioritized. Distributed teams especially need extra feedback cycles before the iteration starts.

list.item: Learn story details — consider all viewpoints (business, end user, production support, programmer)
Write task cards — include testing tasks alongside coding tasks
Decide workload — what can the team realistically commit to?
Ensure stories are testable — if you can't test it, you can't call it done
Write high-level tests and examples with customers during planning
Review tests with customers to verify they capture desired behavior
Review tests with programmers to ensure they're automatable and aligned with code design
Test cases become living documentation of system behavior
Start simple — test the happy path first, then add complexity
Assess risk — identify variations, edge cases, and fragile areas
Coding and testing progress together — not separate activities
Power of Three — programmer, tester, and customer discuss each feature
Focus on one story at a time
Pair testing — tester and programmer test together at one workstation
"Show Me" — demonstrate working features to customers early
Talk to customers — clarify requirements as questions arise
Understand the business — learn the customer's daily work
Is it a defect or a feature? — discuss with the customer
Zero bug tolerance — fix bugs found during the iteration immediately
Decide which bugs to log — if shown to programmer and fixed right away, maybe no need
Choose when to fix — critical bugs now; others as stories in the backlog
Technical debt accumulates when bugs are deferred
Keep the build green — highest priority when a test fails
Keep the build quick — address slowdowns immediately
Build the regression suite incrementally — add tests for each story
Check the "big picture" — don't forget system-wide testing
Iteration metrics: track progress with burndown charts, test pass rates, defect trends

section.paragraph: Demonstrate completed stories to stakeholders. Get feedback. This is a form of critiquing the product — business users see what was delivered and revise priorities.

list.item: Start, Stop, Continue — what should we start doing, stop doing, continue doing?
Focus on one or two improvements at a time
Improvement ideas become task cards for the next iteration
Testers raise testing-related issues for the whole team to address
Celebrate successes — acknowledgment is important for change to stick

key-box.heading: Key Retrospective Practice
key-box.paragraph: The team uses yesterday's experience to do a better job tomorrow. This is a way for the team to provide feedback to itself for continual improvement. Keep an impediment backlog for persistent issues.

list.item: Plan enough time for testing — don't squeeze testing at the end
Test on a staging environment that mirrors production
Final nonfunctional testing (performance, security)
Integration with external applications
Data conversion and database updates
Installation testing
UAT — customers workout new features; gather new story ideas
Alpha/Beta testing — broader user feedback before general release
Release acceptance criteria — define what "ready to ship" means
Communication with all stakeholders about what's included and what's outstanding
Understand impact to business — production support, training, documentation

key-box.heading: SF1: Use the Whole-Team Approach
key-box.paragraph: Quality is everyone's responsibility. Testers, programmers, customers, and specialists collaborate constantly. Any team member can take on any testing task.

key-box.heading: SF2: Adopt an Agile Testing Mind-Set
key-box.paragraph: Customer-focused, results-oriented, collaborative, eager to learn. Attitude counts more than skill. Drive development with tests, not documents.

key-box.heading: SF3: Automate Regression Testing
key-box.paragraph: No agile team succeeds with only manual testing. Follow the test automation pyramid. Automate at the lowest level possible. Keep builds green and fast.

key-box.heading: SF4: Provide and Obtain Feedback
key-box.paragraph: Continuous feedback drives the project forward. Fast builds, big visible charts, demos, retrospectives. The tester's role as information provider is central.

key-box.heading: SF5: Build a Foundation of Core Practices

list.item: Continuous integration with automated builds
Reliable test environments
Manage technical debt aggressively
Work incrementally — thin slices, small chunks
Coding and testing are part of one process
Synergy between practices — they reinforce each other

key-box.heading: SF6: Collaborate with Customers
key-box.paragraph: Close involvement throughout each iteration. Help them express requirements as tests. Achieve advance clarity. One voice of the customer.

key-box.heading: SF7: Look at the Big Picture
key-box.paragraph: Don't get lost in individual stories. Consider system-wide impact, ripple effects, nonfunctional requirements. Use all four testing quadrants as a checklist.

chapter.title: Agile Testing — Practical Daily Work Guide

list.item: Quality is a team sport — never say "that's the tester's job." Every team member owns quality. Raise testing issues in stand-ups and retrospectives.
Attitude over skill — be collaborative, curious, and proactive. Sit with developers, invite yourself to meetings, ask questions constantly.
Tests are specifications — write tests to define what the code should do, not just to verify it after the fact. Tests drive development.
Embrace change — requirements will change. Automation is the safety net that makes change sustainable.
Fail fast, learn faster — have courage to experiment. Short iterations give you frequent chances to evaluate and improve.

key-box.heading: The Power of Three
key-box.paragraph: For every feature discussion, include a programmer, a tester, and the product owner. This prevents hidden assumptions, missed requirements, and communication breakdowns. If you see two people discussing a feature, butt in.

key-box.heading: Thin Slices First
key-box.paragraph: Always start with the simplest happy path that goes end-to-end. Get it working, then add complexity layer by layer. Pattern: write test → write code → run tests → learn → repeat.

list.item: "What's the worst thing that could happen?" — identifies risks
"What's the best thing that could happen?" — defines the happy path
"How do we know we're done?" — establishes acceptance criteria
"What parts of the system does this story touch?" — reveals ripple effects
"What are the post conditions?" — uncovers hidden side effects

key-box.heading: Use the Quadrants as a Daily Checklist
key-box.paragraph: For every story, ask: Do we have unit tests (Q1)? Business-facing tests (Q2)? Have we done exploratory testing (Q3)? Have we considered performance and security (Q4)? Use "stock" task cards on the board to remind the team.

list.item: Follow the pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration/API tests, minimal GUI tests
Push tests to the lowest level — if you can test at the API level, don't test through the GUI
Builds must be fast — unit build under 10 minutes; if it creeps up, fix it immediately
Never comment out a failing test — fix the build. "Tests are not temporary!"
Tests are code — refactor them, review them, version control them
Start automating today — even a simple smoke test suite adds enormous value
Face-to-face over tools — walk to someone's desk instead of sending a message. Use candy dishes to attract developers.
Show, don't tell — demo working features to customers early and often. Do informal demos mid-iteration.
Pair test — sit with a programmer to reproduce bugs faster than writing a defect report
Use examples as a common language — spreadsheets, whiteboard drawings, mock-ups bridge the gap between business and technical teams
Big visible charts — display test counts, build status, burndown charts where everyone can see

section.h2: Dealing with Bugs and Technical Debt

list.item: Zero bug tolerance within the iteration — if you find a bug during development, fix it now
Talk before logging — show the bug to the programmer directly; write a unit test to reproduce it; fix it. Skip the DTS when possible.
Track technical debt — advocate for "engineering sprints" to pay it down. Frame it in ROI terms for management.
Use retrospectives — if bugs are leaking to production, ask the team "what can we do differently?"
Keep an impediment backlog — track what's slowing the team down; prioritize and address items
Experiment constantly — try a new tool, technique, or practice for 2-3 iterations; evaluate results in retrospectives
Learn continuously — attend user groups, read blogs, join mailing lists, learn a scripting language
Celebrate small wins — gold stars, high-fives, team lunches. Acknowledgment makes change stick.
Be a change agent — use patterns from Fearless Change: "Ask for Help," "Brown Bag," "Guru on Your Side"

row.cell: Measures team's ability to deliver value repeatedly
Indicates code health and feedback speed
Shows growing safety net over time
Measures whether quality practices are working
Tracks iteration progress; flags problems early

list.item: Never use metrics to judge individual performance
Don't measure lines of code or defects per person
Don't collect metrics you won't act on
Focus on the goal, not the number

section.h2: Quick Reference: What to Do Monday Morning

list.item: Invite yourself to the planning meeting if you haven't been
Ask the product owner for examples of the next story's desired behavior
Propose the "Power of Three" rule to your team
Write one automated test for the happy path of the current story
Check: is there a continuous build running? If not, make it your first task to set one up
Do 30 minutes of exploratory testing on the latest build — take notes
At the retrospective, raise one testing improvement idea
Start doing it — today!

Ch 6: The Purpose of Testing — Agile Testing Quadrants
Ch 7: Technology-Facing Tests that Support the Team
Ch 8: Business-Facing Tests that Support the Team
Ch 9: Toolkit for Business-Facing Tests
Ch 10: Business-Facing Tests that Critique the Product
Ch 11: Critiquing the Product Using Technology-Facing Tests
Ch 12: Summary of Testing Quadrants
Ch 13: Why Automate and What Holds Us Back
Ch 14: An Agile Test Automation Strategy
Ch 15: Tester Activities in Release Planning
Ch 16: Hit the Ground Running
Ch 17: Iteration Kickoff
Ch 18: Coding and Testing
Ch 19: Wrap Up the Iteration
Ch 20: Successful Delivery
Ch 21: Key Success Factors
Agent Summary: Practical Daily Work Guide

syllabus.footer-text: Agile Testing: A Practical Guide for Testers and Agile Teams — Lisa Crispin & Janet Gregory (2009)
